title: वायु स्तोत्रम्
devanagariText:
॥ श्री वायु स्तोत्रम् ॥

प्राणेश्वरं जगत्प्राणं सर्वप्राणिहिताय च।
वायुदेवं नमस्यामि प्राणदं प्राणधारकम्॥ १॥

सप्तलोकचरं देवं सर्वव्यापिनमव्ययम्।
गन्धवाहं सुखस्पर्शं वायुं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ २॥

यस्मात्प्राणाः प्रभवन्ति यस्मादपानमुच्यते।
व्यानोदानसमानश्च वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ३॥

पवनं पवमानं च मातरिश्वानमुत्तमम्।
मरुतां पतिमीशानं वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ४॥

सर्वभूतान्तरस्थं च सर्वजीवनदायकम्।
अनिलं शीघ्रगामिं च वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ५॥

ऋग्वेदे प्रतिपादितं यजुर्वेदविभूषितम्।
सामगानप्रियं नित्यं वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ६॥

शब्दस्पर्शगुणोपेतं दिक्पालं पश्चिमां दिशम्।
नीलवर्णं महाबाहुं वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ७॥

हनूमत्पितरं वन्दे भीमसेनस्य कारणम्।
बलप्रदं सुखप्रदं वायुं तं प्रणमाम्यहम्॥ ८॥

॥ इति श्री वायु स्तोत्रम् सम्पूर्णम् ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Vayu Stotram ||

Praneshvaram Jagatpranam Sarvapranihitaya Cha |
Vayudevam Namasyami Pranadam Pranadharakam || 1 ||

Saptalokaacharam Devam Sarvavyapinam Avyayam |
Gandhavaham Sukhasparshan Vayum Pranamamy Aham || 2 ||

Yasmat Pranah Prabhavanti Yasmad Apanam Uchyate |
Vyanodana Samanash Cha Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 3 ||

Pavanam Pavamanam Cha Matarishvanam Uttamam |
Marutam Patim Ishanam Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 4 ||

Sarvabhutantarastham Cha Sarvajivanadayakam |
Anilam Shighragaminam Cha Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 5 ||

Rigvede Pratipaditam Yajurveda Vibhushitam |
Samagana Priyam Nityam Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 6 ||

Shabdasparsha Gunopetam Dikpalam Pashchimam Disham |
Nilavarnam Mahababum Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 7 ||

Hanumatpitaram Vande Bhimasenasya Karanam |
Balapradam Sukhapradam Vayum Tam Pranamamy Aham || 8 ||

|| Iti Shri Vayu Stotram Sampurnam ||
hindiMeaning:
प्राणेश्वर, जगत के प्राण, सर्व प्राणियों के हित में लगे, प्राणदाता, प्राणधारक वायुदेव को नमस्कार।

सप्त लोकों में विचरण करने वाले, सर्वव्यापी, अव्यय, गन्धवाह, सुखस्पर्शी वायु को प्रणाम।

जिनसे प्राण, अपान, व्यान, उदान, समान — पंचप्राण उत्पन्न होते हैं, उन वायु को प्रणाम।

पवन, पवमान, मातरिश्वा, मरुतों के पति, ईशान वायु को प्रणाम।

सर्व भूतों में अन्तःस्थ, सर्वजीवनदायक, अनिल, शीघ्रगामी वायु को प्रणाम।

हनुमान के पिता, भीमसेन के कारणभूत, बलप्रद, सुखप्रद वायु को प्रणाम।
benefits: Eight verses praising Vayu Deva as the cosmic life-force (Prana)
Invokes all five Pranas through worship of Vayu
Invokes vital energy, health, strength and longevity
Special mention of Vayu as father of Hanuman and Bhima
Ideal for health, breathing disorders and increasing vitality
description: Dedicated to Lord Vayu, the Vayu Stotram (वायु स्तोत्रम्) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Rooted in Vayu Purana, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Devotees recite this prayer to invoke all five Pranas through worship of Vayu and special mention of Vayu as father of Hanuman and Bhima. It is also said to grant vital energy, health, strength and longevity. Recitation is particularly auspicious on Saturday (Shanivar). Comprising 8 verses, it can be completed in approximately 4 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Vayu Stotram in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Vayu Stotram and which scripture preserves it?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Stotram (वायु स्तोत्रम्) is an eight-verse Sanskrit hymn of praise to Lord Vayu — the Vedic deity of wind, breath, and the cosmic life-force (prana). It is preserved in the Vayu Purana stotra tradition and is also venerated in the Madhva (Dvaita Vedanta) Sampradaya, where Vayu is theologically identified as Mukhya Prana (the chief among the gods after Vishnu) with three avataras: Hanuman (Treta yuga), Bhima (Dvapara yuga), and Madhvacharya (Kali yuga). The eight verses praise Vayu as Praneshvara (lord of prana), Sapta-loka-chara (mover through seven worlds), source of the pancha-pranas (prana, apana, vyana, udana, samana), Pavana, Pavamana, Matarishvan, the lord of the Maruts, the western direction-guardian (paschima dik-pala), and explicitly as the father of Hanuman and the cause of Bhima's birth — culminating in the prayer for bala (strength) and sukha (happiness). (Sources: Vayu Purana; Madhva Vayustuti tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: Why is Vayu called the father of Hanuman and Bhima?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 8 of the Vayu Stotram explicitly invokes Vayu as Hanumat-pitaram vande Bhimasenasya karanam — the father of Hanuman and the cause of Bhimasena. The Hanuman connection is from the Ramayana and Skanda Purana: Anjana, the wife of Kesari, performed tapas to obtain a divine son and Vayu blessed her with the wind-essence by which Hanuman (also called Vayuputra, Maruti, Pavanaputra) was conceived; Hanuman thus inherited Vayu's vegavana (swiftness) and bala (immense strength). The Bhima connection is from the Mahabharata: when Pandu's wife Kunti invoked Vayu using the Durvasa mantra during the Pandavas' birth, Vayu fathered Bhimasena, who likewise inherited his father's tremendous physical strength and was therefore called Maruti and Vayuputra. In Madhva Sampradaya theology, Hanuman, Bhima and Madhvacharya himself are recognized as the three avataras of Vayu in the three yugas (Treta, Dvapara, Kali). (Sources: Valmiki Ramayana; Mahabharata Adi Parva; Madhva Sampradaya theology.)

seoFaqs.question: When should the Vayu Stotram be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Stotram is most powerful when recited on Saturday (Shanivar) — traditionally associated with prana-shakti and Hanuman worship — and is also recited on Tuesdays (Hanuman day, since Vayu is Hanuman's father). It is especially recommended during pranayama practice and yoga sadhana, on Hanuman Jayanti, Bhima Jayanti (Magha Shukla Ekadashi), Madhva Jayanti / Madhva Navami (Vilamba Vijaya Dashami in Madhva tradition), Mahabharata Pravachana days, and at the start of any breathing/health regimen. Devotees with respiratory difficulties (asthma, breathing disorders), low energy, weakness or who require physical strength, athletes, military personnel, yoga practitioners and sadhakas of pranayama all chant it daily. The recitation at dawn — when the prana-vayu is most pure — is the prescribed time. (Sources: Vayu Purana; traditional Madhva Sampradaya recitation calendar.)

seoFaqs.question: What are the pancha pranas mentioned in the Vayu Stotram?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 3 of the Vayu Stotram declares: Yasmat pranah prabhavanti yasmad apanam uchyate, vyanodana samanash cha vayum tam pranamamy aham — the Vayu from whom prana, apana, vyana, udana and samana arise. These are the pancha-vayus or pancha-pranas — five vital airs governing all bodily and cognitive functions: (1) Prana — the upward-moving life-breath in the chest, governing inhalation, the heart, and the senses; (2) Apana — the downward-moving force in the lower abdomen, governing excretion, urination, menstruation and childbirth; (3) Vyana — the all-pervading current circulating throughout the body, governing circulation, nervous coordination and movement; (4) Udana — the upward force in the throat, governing speech, swallowing, and the rise of consciousness at death; (5) Samana — the equalising force in the navel region, governing digestion and assimilation. Mastery of the pancha-pranas through pranayama is the foundation of yoga. (Sources: Prashna Upanishad; Brihadaranyaka Upanishad; Hatha Yoga Pradipika.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Vayu Stotram and Madhva Vayustuti?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are Sanskrit hymns to Vayu but differ in length, depth and tradition. The Vayu Stotram is a relatively short eight-verse hymn from the Vayu Purana corpus, accessible to all Hindu traditions, focused on Vayu's cosmic functions (prana, sapta-loka movement, direction-guardian, lord of Maruts) with concluding mention of his fatherhood of Hanuman and Bhima — suitable for daily householder recitation. The Madhva Vayustuti (also called Sri Vayustuti) is a much longer 41-verse stotra composed by Sri Trivikrama Panditacharya in the 13th century, extolling Vayu in his three Madhva-recognized avataras — Hanuman, Bhima, and Madhvacharya — with deep Dvaita Vedanta theology, very dense and difficult Sanskrit, and is the daily prayer of the Madhva Sampradaya. The Vayu Stotram is the entry-level prayer; the Vayustuti is the advanced sadhaka's daily parayana. (Sources: Vayu Purana; Sri Vayustuti by Trivikrama Panditacharya.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does daily recitation of Vayu Stotram grant?
seoFaqs.answer: The Vayu Stotram is recited for the following blessings: (1) Prana-shakti — strengthening of vital life-force and breathing capacity, (2) bala — physical strength like Hanuman and Bhima, (3) arogya — robust health and freedom from respiratory disorders (asthma, bronchitis, low oxygen), (4) ayushya — long life through balanced pancha-prana flow, (5) viryam and tejas — vitality, vigour and brilliance, (6) protection from vata-dosha imbalances and nervous disorders in Ayurveda, (7) success in pranayama, yoga sadhana and athletic pursuits, (8) the grace of Hanuman (Vayuputra) and Bhima through their father Vayu, (9) speed and swiftness in undertakings (Vayu is shighra-gami), (10) for Madhva devotees, the supreme blessing of Vishnu via Vayu as the jiva-uttama (foremost of jivas). The stotra is particularly prescribed by traditional Vaidyas for chronic respiratory conditions. (Sources: Vayu Purana; traditional Ayurveda and yoga recommendations.)
